paragraphs.text:
Notes and context
- Transferrin binding is Fe(III)‑ and carbonate‑dependent; published constants are highly sensitive to carbonate/bicarbonate, pH (≈7–8), temperature, and ionic strength. SRD‑46 contains carbonate complexes of Fe(III) with small ligands, but not protein partners.
- If you’re aiming for physiological comparisons, it’s best to compare constants measured at matched temperature and ionic strength with carbonate present; otherwise, transferrin affinities from the literature are not directly comparable to small‑ligand data.

claims.text: Notes and context
Transferrin binding is Fe(III)‑ and carbonate‑dependent;
published constants are highly sensitive to carbonate/bicarbonate, pH (≈7–8), temperature, and ionic strength.
SRD‑46 contains carbonate complexes of Fe(III) with small ligands,
but not protein partners.
If you’re aiming for physiological comparisons,
it’s best to compare constants measured at matched temperature and ionic strength with carbonate present;
otherwise,
transferrin affinities from the literature are not directly comparable to small‑ligand data.

Established biochemistry: transferrin requires Fe(III) and carbonate as a synergistic anion; not present in SRD‑46 rows here.
Established: protein metal-binding constants depend on carbonate/bicarbonate, pH 7–8, temperature, ionic strength; not directly stated in SRD‑46 rows.
No Fe(III) + carbonate entries were found; Step 13 shows hydrogen carbonate with Cd2+, Ca2+, Tl+ but not Fe3+.
